And you're the one that's working on that. And you're the one that's working on that. And you're the one that's working on that. And so it's been the most powerful thing that I've been in my life. And so now I'm here to want to know how do we make it bigger and more affordable? Because you and I, we don't look like I'm in a jail. Yeah, you know, I drop that bomb all the time. It's some people Google right away and they, you know, they can tell me about me. First of all, thank you for doing. I love you. Thank you for doing what you're doing. I'm a big fan. I was like hustling diamonds back then. Oh, you were? Thanks. So, hey, that's why I don't, that business sucks. You know, listen, I started What's Next to be this hub that would, so we could bring the groups together to collaborate. 'Cause there's a lot of use, there's more power in numbers, right? So I wanted to bring everybody together. That was my goal. That's why the Department of Corrections and Correctional Industries were supportive. And they'll come in and support this. So, you know, you really, I should introduce you to Correctional Industries because they'll support you and help connect the dots and probably. Sorry, sorry. Yeah. I do wanna make a difference. Yeah. Absolutely. We can connect afterwards. Let's get a question. Go ahead. You talked about being good at finding locations and dialing in to what the consumer wants in your category. Mm-hm. Can you expand more on that process, what that's like for you? So, okay. Well, in locations, that was really retail. You know, that was what upon exchange was all about. I mean, I kind of had a good feel for where it belongs. So it was on... It wasn't... Even my store in Bellevue did really well, where everybody said you can't open a store in Bellevue. It killed it. Because it was just... There were people coming in, borrowing 20,000. Different people for different reasons, right? So I had a good sense of what that is. In food, do I have that sense? I don't know. Probably not. In products, for Hoi Fong, look, whatever... It's just a golden brand. It's iconic, okay? I could slap... I almost had to deal with Valspar to do paints, okay? I mean, they buy pajamas at Target. I can slap that logo and that iconic brand on something. It's going to sell, all right? For Good Planet, it was Culinary Co. And it was... Really, the product was killer and people liked it. But it wasn't until I understood the category. It's not just vegans. It's not just those who are lactose intolerant. It's not those who have allergens, right? Because we're allergen-free, we're coconut milk or coconut oil-based. But I learned that a huge number of the people buying these plant-based products are doing it because it's good for the planet. One of the biggest impact on our environment and our ozone layer is the dairy industry. I didn't know that when I jumped in. When I figured it out, it's Good Planet. It's plant-based. Smalley, you see the plant, you see the planet. It's good for the planet. It makes a connection, right? It has a story I can tell. So it was having that... I mean, my competitors are Violife, Daya, let's see, Follow Your Heart, okay? So, you know, my message to me was much broader and made a connection with the consumer. So it's really understanding that consumer, that segment. You want to connect with the consumer. Yeah. You want your product. It's got to be great. And I've got to tell you, I mean, our product is, you know, hands down the best product in the market. It's very competitive, but the brand is what's going to connect with the consumer, the buyers, and it's worked. Yeah. So, yeah, I think you really have to understand your category. You've got to connect the dots, right? I mean, not just for yourself. I mean, a lot of people will do messaging that is all about them, okay? But it can't be all about you. I mean, what's all about you is putting out a great product and being successful and helping whatever your goal is, right? It's better for you for the consumer, better for you for the planet, whatever it is, okay? But the message, I mean, Tina's, you know, great cookie. I mean, sorry. Well, actually, that might work, Tina's great cookies. That's a bad example. You know, but if you call it Barbie's because that's your kid's name, you know, then that's it. It doesn't work. It's got to be a connection to the consumer and the buyer. Are there other food entrepreneurs that have inspired you or do inspire you? Well, I mean, God, I don't want to sound like a jerk. No, you know what, listen. I mean, I look what's going on in the market, okay? And yes, I've been inspired by people and their success and what they've overcome and how well they've done, okay? I mean, Angie's Boom Chicka Pop was much more successful than Pop Gourmet. I had a better product, right? But I didn't connect with the private equity group as soon as she did. And I, you know, so she's off to the races and we're still trying to grow, right? But no, I'm just one of those people that I kind of internalize and think through things myself. And I mean, I look at other people's successes and try to figure out how I can apply maybe, you know, what they've done. It may be my category, but I'm not ever trying to replicate somebody in my category. Real quick one, kind of follow-up. Yeah. Have you heard of Chef Gordon, Superman? Has anybody here heard of this? I have not. The guy is Alice Cooper, the rock star's manager, business guy through and through, but he kind of created the celebrity chef movement. Mm-hm. And listening to some of your stuff, I'm kind of from a pure business background and then moving into food and whatnot. Chef is a lot like that, FYI. Oh. I love Superman's in the movie. It's very good. Really? Cool. Thank you. Great question. Go ahead. So I heard you speak a lot about relationships and partnership and trust. Yep. And I was just interested in a couple aspects of that in growing a business. How do you determine whether you can trust people and, you know, the people you want to really partner with and go forward with? Whew. People have been the toughest thing for me. Okay. Growing pop. I hired experts all along the way. Experts in their... I hired two executives from Sahali, because a friend of mine was one of the founders of Sahali. Well, they sold the... Sahali. Sahali snacks. Local company... Those are great nuts. Yeah. They really did well. Who did they sell to? I forget now. Marker. Thank you. Appreciate that. So I hired two of their executives that literally almost put us out of business. Wow. Because, you know, look, I do things different. I need... I hired a lot of people that had experts that didn't have the right advice for what we're trying to do. Okay? What made us successful. Okay? A company that's been solidified and is in the marketplace is different than a fast-growing, innovative company. You can take your time and make sure the next product is just so, just when. You know, for a growing... I wanted things in the marketplace. I didn't care. I went out with the product and a picture of the bag that was coming later. I mean, I just wanted people to know what was coming. I captured their interest. And you know what? They say, "When can you ship?" I can... You place an order. I'll have the bags here in eight weeks. It's off to you. Okay? We just did things really quickly. They didn't like that. I had other people that said, you can't... You know, when I was in O Magazine, okay? Literally a day after it came out, I get a call. And I'm in this really small place in Renton. And I answer the phone. And it's, "Hi, who would I speak to about placing?" I said, "Well, I can help you." "Okay. This is, you know, this is Elizabeth with TJ Maxx. And I want to order your popcorn." I saw it in O Magazine. I'm like, "Okay." I'm like, "Who is this?" "Who's pulling the joke?" "Yeah, this is..." I go, "You guys don't carry food." She goes, "We carry lots of food." I go, "You do?" She goes, "And we have 2,500 stores that I'll put you in." I go, "Okay." She goes, "Can you deliver 2,500 stores?" I go, "Absolutely." "No." We have a loaning doc. I mean, we went from, at that time, we went from 11 employees to 30 employees, like literally in a week, because I took the order. I hung up, and this person I had hired who had 30 years' experience, she came from Starbucks, said, "You can't sell it to TJ Maxx." I said, "Well, why not?" She goes, "Well, if you sell to TJ Maxx, it's a discounter. None of the other specialty stores will want to buy you." I go, "Wow." I go, "Well, I'll tell you what." We'll give it a shot. "No." I said, "You have two hours to come back with a replacement order for 2,500 stores, and I won't ship them." And she goes, "Well, that's not possible." I go, "You're right." So we shipped them, and you know what happened? Honest to God. I mean, she was like, "You're going to be sorry. You're going to be sorry." I'm like, "You know what?" Won't even use her name. I said, "I want to sell product, okay? I have investors. I want to get this product out there. I'm not saying no to 2,500 stores." First of all, our margins were better than any other place at TJ Maxx because they paid more. We don't have a distributor, and they picked it up. So I didn't have freight. I was in the stores a week, and I started getting phone calls from Dean and DeLuca because their buyer was in TJ Maxx and said, "We want to carry your product." And I started getting buyers that way from TJ Maxx, and it grew that way organically. And distributors. I mean, it just took off. She was dead wrong. So I was so ignorant. I didn't know. I just wanted to do business. That way a business worked. Nobody discounted me. I mean, as far as because I was in TJ Maxx. They didn't care. And if you go to TJ Maxx now, everybody's in there. There's not that fear of being in there. Dilettante, they want to grow that business. They're great partners. So I'm hearing what you're saying about trust is like, one, you really trust yourself. Have to. And you really trust when you've got a good feeling about something and stuff happens, and you're excited about it, and you want to do it, you put your word on doing it. You're just going to do it. Yeah. And if somebody can bring something that's more exciting than that and bring you a better feeling that's also exciting to you, yeah, I think you'll go with that. But if they're just like raining on your parade. Yeah. Like, hey. Look, it's your dream. And just let me clarify this a bit. Okay. The relationships I have with companies, those are different, okay? You know, I mean, now I'm dealing with crafts and Kellogg's and stuff like that. And all these different, they're all individual. You have to respect how they do business, okay? And you build a mutual trust and it's based on integrity, okay? Hoi Fung is obviously a unique one. So I've learned a lot. People that you're bringing into your own culture have to be a good fit. They need to earn your trust. It's a two-way street, right? I mean, the four guys that are with me now, well, one just got out of prison. I met him in an entrepreneurial program that I put on there. So he's there and he's a -- I keep saying he's a killer. He's not a killer, but he's killing it, okay? I mean -- He's killing it. Yeah. I go, this guy is a killer. I'm like, whoa, really? I'm like, no, no. I didn't mean it that way. The other three guys are people that have worked with me from almost day one at Pop Grommet. One was my VP of sales. He came with me. The other one was -- his name is Sachin. He was my second employee. He grew into a VP position. He's there with me. And the other woman, she was like my third employee. So we built trust. And I know that they're going to support me. And they know that I'm just going to go do it. I mean, they can laugh at me a lot. I mean, I got a lot of quirks, right? I mean, they're like, you can't do that. I'm like, well, I'm going to try it. So they get who I am. But we get who each other are and we support each other to be successful. That's what you need, okay? I mean, resumes -- sorry. You know, I think the most important -- our resume is important in financial, you know, CFOs. But they also have to support your goals. They have to understand your goals, your culture. It's very important, right? You can't just trust guys and say, all the guys that said they knew how to run a plant -- I mean, I might as well just shot myself. We don't even have a plant. PopCormade does not have a production plant anymore. Okay? We built four. And the last one cost us about $9 million. Down the toilet. Okay? I trusted the wrong people. I believed in resumes. It doesn't work. Can we add on a high note? Sure. Can you talk to me about some of the rock stars, the experiences that you've had with people? Well, you know what? I mean, literally can run around and pinch myself with the guys that -- the people that I had brought in that never had jobs or their first jobs out of high school, a lot of these guys that were transitioning are so intelligent they just never knew how to direct that energy and how to apply it or no one would ever trust them. One of my lead investors, his son, he dropped out of college. I mean, he's a very wealthy family. He's like, "Hey, will you give him a shot?" I said, "Sure." The guy came to me. He goes, "Look, my dad's friend over here put me in the warehouse. My dad's friend over there had me doing clerk jobs." I go, "Not here. It works the same way here for everybody. I want you to try to get involved in everything. And we're going to figure out where your strengths are. And that's how we're going to apply it." So I think I see a lot of people go on and do great things. One of the guys went on and he's on the board at What's Next. And he was the first job he ever had outside of prison. And now he's helped running an organization. So just the connections that I've made across the board. Me learning not to be fearful of who I am or being insecure or having an ego. I mean, you know, ego should just go out the window, right? I just love doing this and I love connecting with people, which is why when I go to trade shows, I am behind that booth. I mean, I just go, I leave literally. The only reason I leave is to go to the restroom, okay? I don't walk around the shows. I like connecting with the buyers. I like collecting with it because I like the feedback. I mean, it's thrilling to me. So, you know, just learning how to deal with people and all the people that have grown with me, it's amazing. It really is. I mean, this whole thing, I travel around the world and it's just, I mean, I never thought, I mean, who would think I have partners in a plant in Russia or building a plant in India or these guys in Japan? I mean, I was out of prison, you know, where nobody even wanted to talk to me, okay? I was isolated. And, you know, it's just a great place to see that you can change your life. It's up to you, right? It doesn't matter the cards you're dealt, where you come from, what you've been through, what anybody thinks of you. If you decide to live a good life and do things right and you want to be successful and you put your, you know, you're really committed and honest and have integrity, you know? It all works out. It sure does. Yeah, absolutely works out. Well, David, you've been absolutely amazing. Well, thank you. Awesome. I appreciate your sharing that story. It's a great one and just your honesty and forthrightness. It's amazing. Thank you. Thank you, Mike. You're awesome. It's great to be here. Yeah, thanks. Thank you.

segments.text: Welcome to the Startup Grind.
Without any further ado, I'd like to welcome David Israel, our guest tonight.
That's what made you run, right? Where do you want me?
Thank you.
It's funny, David, because there's been a couple guests where that was the scariest part of the night.
I was like, oh no, I don't want these people to have to do that.
So yeah, that's what we do.
But again, for those of you who have not been to the Startup Grind, it really is just a conversation between David and I.
It's just a discussion about his experiences, what he's done, obviously around startups and starting a company or companies because he's started several.
And so we'll have an interview for 45-ish minutes, and then we'll have 15 minutes of Q&A.
So hold your questions at the end.
Feel free to get up, grab wine, beer, food, drink.
You're not going to interrupt us.
Obviously, it's videotaped, so it'll be online in about a week or so, 10 days.
Yeah, that's it.
So again, thanks for coming.
I'm glad to be here.
Honored to be here.
Appreciate it.
Yeah, yeah.
So the reason why we connected was, one, you have an interesting background.
I do.
But two, the volume of work that you've done is pretty interesting.

You know, why not, you know, I don't necessarily care about some of the things like I did specifically, but kind of give us a general idea of the last three or four companies that you have started and have grown.
I mean, the last two have been, you're still working on actually.
We're still working on quite a few.
I mean, I think my biggest, so my first big company was called Pond Exchange.
It's not one that I'm really proud of today.
I mean, it was a very successful company.
It did exactly what I wanted to do.
And kind of, I mean, can I take a step back?

So when I was about, oh, I think I was 26 or 27, and my wife and I had just been married, we moved, we came back to Seattle, and a friend of mine said, who had sold a large chain of jewelry stores, he asked me to come in and be his partner.
And I knew nothing really about the business, but we opened up, or he opened up, in a place called Aurora Village at the time.
Back in the-
So this was what year?
Late, late 80s.
Yeah.
And we moved in, and everybody else started moving out.
Nordstrom's closed, what's called Frederick and Nelson's, which is another great department store here.
They moved out, Sears shut down, everybody's leaving, and it's becoming this ghost town.
And for me, at the time, I mean, I had made some money doing some deals, and I put everything I had into this thing.
And I was like, oh my God, this is not good.
So, turns out, you know, we signed a five-year lease, and we were there for maybe nine months or a year.
And we're, like, the last four months, I'm just, like, ready to pull my hair out, my wife's ready to kill me.
And, well, our five-year lease turned out to be worth a lot, because everybody's moving out because they're not renewing leases, because they want to build a Costco and a Home Depot there.
Oh, really?
Yes.
So, all of a sudden, you know, the developer comes to us and says, hey, you know what, we need to take you out of your lease.
So, I have no idea what I'm doing.
My partner, who's from a very successful family, he really didn't know what he was doing either, but he was pretty arrogant.
So, he put a price tag out there, and you know what?
They took it.
So, I turned out, you know, walking away with a pretty good chunk of money for a guy who was about to lose everything, and all these jewelry stores started to call me up.
Thought you knew what you were doing.
No, they wanted to borrow money.

Because jewelry stores, if you're not a major chain, you're a family-run business, and it's really difficult to get financing, because inventory control, management, I mean, banks aren't really financing you.
Interesting.
So, they came to me, and I started to loan them money, but I wanted collateral, because I didn't trust them.

That led to coin shops, then precious metal dealers, and then pawn shops, and I was loaning money to pawn shops, well, and all these other folks up and down the I-5 corridor, and I was like, how do these guys make money?
They treat their customers like crap.
Their stores look like crap.
Everything's overpriced.
I mean, their hours suck, and it was one day I read in Fortune Magazine about this company.

There was a company called Cash America out of Dallas, Texas that went public, and they were getting all these accolades, and they were buying old mom-and-pop shops and consolidating them and turning them into retail presents, right?
Shirts and ties, you know, really nice stores, you know, everything was priced right, and they were going haywire, and they said they were going to grow.
So I was like, well, this is something I could do.
So I wrote a business plan with the help of Deloitte Touche, and then decided that's what I was into.
Went down to Cash America, looked at all their stores in Dallas, you know, took pictures, came back, replicated it here.
And, look, I just thought, at the time, I knew it was a slam-dunk easy deal, kind of brainless.
It wasn't.
I mean, I didn't know what I was doing, so I could loan money on jewelry, but my first loan happened to be a Sawzall.
So I was like, uh-oh.
You know, how do I test that to see if it's real, right?
So, anyway, it did exactly what I wanted.
The goal was to build this chain and sell it to Cash America, and we built it from one store in 1993.
We had about 16 stores by 1996.
You know, Howard Schultz, Jeff Brodman, all the, the majority of all the top executives at Starbucks and Costco and their boards were my investors.
So I raised about $4 million in 96, and I encountered a little hiccup, but that later, about eight years later, nine years later, sold to Cash America for about $80 million.
So it did exactly what I wanted, but there were some other things along the way.
Yeah, exactly.
So, so tell us, like, in that process of, like, the sale of the company, which is always a, it's a, I mean, I went through three myself.
They're super hard.
They're super frustrating normally.
Like, what were the things that you could have done better?
Well, we could have held out, number one.
Well, sure.
Because the value of our business was getting stronger.
But the thing was, is by the nine years later or 10 years later, I had stepped aside because of, which I'm sure we'll get to in cover, some hiccups in my process there.
Look, I think we did everything exactly right, except hold out for, you know, hold our ground for a higher dollar number.
Yeah.
So you, you just, other than that, you would have, like, just said, you know, let's wait, wait a few more years.
It was so profitable.
I mean, that business is very lucrative if you operate properly.
We made it even more profitable because we were managing our, our money and our finances.
And we figured out the formula of how to really be successful was, I mean, loans are great.
But when you convert those loans into a retail sale, which isn't a lot, by the way, I mean, it's about a 25%, 25% of the people don't come back if they get a loan.
Or they might be there selling you something.
So, but when you convert that inventory and you clean it and you polish it up and you, you merchandise it properly, we're getting a lot more than our competitors were.
You know, probably twice as much.
And we could offer layaway.
I mean, we were well-financed because we were completely, you know, we had banks, you know, chomping at the bit to be our, our bank.
Yeah, because we knew what we were doing.
It was all, we had, you know, all of the financial management in place.
We had our, our, all of our products were inventoried and put away.
So, you know, we knew exactly what we were doing.
Yeah.
So, so talk to us about this food business that you're, yeah, it's funny.
We tried to get together for coffee before this event, probably what, at least four or five times.
Yep.
Every time I'd email him, he'd respond back.
Oh, no, I'm flying a blah, blah, blah, blah tomorrow.
And tomorrow you're flying again.
I fly again tomorrow.
He's traveling like a crazy man.
So tell us about the food business that really is what's going on today in your life.
And, you know, I know it's doing well.
Give us, give us a hint what, what it is.
So, well, I started a company called Pop Gourmet, which started out as a popcorn company.
And that just changed my life because, not just because of where I originated from, but because, look, I'm just the kind of guy, I like to move really fast.
I don't, obstacles, I'm just like, you know, I'll figure it out later.
And I don't like the word no.
I don't like the word I can't, words I can't.
But it turned out that food seemed to be the right fit for me at the right time because innovation was lacking.
It doesn't matter what category I went into.
But as soon as I figured out popcorn, I started saying, okay, I need to replicate this.
And I need to move into chips and then condiments and then all these other things.
So, last February, I stepped away from Pop Gourmet as a CEO.
I resigned because I had different views than the chairman.
And, look, I just, because of my history, I picked up some bad habits, which I don't like to be bullied.
And if someone tries to bully me, I just, I have a couple of choice words, which I'm not proud of all the time.
I mean, I got to tell you, I mean, I wish that really wasn't kind of part of my temperament, but it is.
But I try to do the right thing all the time.
But I decided to move on and do what I'm passionate about, and that is being innovative and trying to disrupt categories.
So, I had formed a relationship with a company called Hoifang that does a sauce called Sriracha.
And in 2013, when I reached out to David Tran, who was the founder, they had never, ever partnered with anybody before.
Never.
And I just happened to have the right story at the right time.
You know, I was doing all these really cool products with my popcorn, and I did Almond Roque.
I partnered with them.
I partnered with a company called Rogue Blue Creamery out of Oregon to do this really high-end blue cheese popcorn.
And Oprah was a big fan of mine.
And we were doing all these great things.
And I thought, well, you know what?
I partnered with a company here in Seattle called Bacon Salt.
Oh, the Bacon Salt.
Yeah.
And they were doing really well.
I thought, wow, this is a great collaboration.
So, Dave Lefkow and I had partnered, and I used his seasoning, and I launched Simply Bacon.
And you know what?
It was the one product that was a great product, by the way.
But what it was on, people bought it.
I either got an email that we love it, or I got an email that we hate it, and we're not buying any more of your products.
And not being from the food industry, I was still smart enough to understand that that wasn't good news.
I mean, if somebody doesn't like your product, you don't want it to be offensive enough.
They're not going to buy anything else.
So, I was seeing this 50-50 thing for a while.
I mean, quite a bit.
And I decided I can't have Bacon Salt anymore.
So, I went down to tell Dave that I really appreciated him trusting me with his brand,
and I didn't have to discontinue his popcorn, because I had to find something that really connected with the consumer better.
And he goes, like, what?
I said, well, you know, I don't know.
I'm thinking about reaching out to the Sriracha folks.
I left his office.
Five months later, he has a product on the shelf, and it's a hoi feng.
Well, it's not a hoi feng.
It's a Sriracha popcorn with a rooster and a green cap, and it's got the bottle on the front.
And I was like, oh, my God.
I did control my temper, and I wrote him an email.
I go, you know what?
That's awful.
That's pretty unethical, but shame on me.
You know?
I shouldn't have been broadcasting my idea.
Well, four months later, I find out that he's not partnered with him.
He's just knocked him off.
And my branding guy drafts this email, and he designed a bag, and it said it had the real hoi feng design on it, and it had my logo on the front.
He goes, you should try to reach out to these guys.
He goes, they don't want to do business with anybody, but it's worth a shot.
I'm like, oh, I don't know.
I don't really need a battle.
And, you know, so I just decided one day I was looking at my, I kept pulling it up and looking at this cool bag, and I was like, oh, what the heck?
So I called down there.
No, it's impossible to reach anybody.
I send an email.
There's only info at hoi feng, and they don't respond for a couple weeks.
So I just packed up a box of all my products, and I wrote them a letter, and I go, let me tell you about Popform A.
Let me tell you about me, and let me tell you a story about this other company.
That I dealt with in Seattle.
I said, I think you should allow me to bring the real sriracha popcorn to the consumer.
And I don't know, I sent it off, and a week later, I get this email, and the subject line was sriracha popcorn.
And I was like, oh, shit.
Came in like at 9.30 in the morning.
I'm like, I'm not opening this, because I don't want to ruin my day.
And my whole office is like, open and open.
I'm like, no, can't do it.
So I think I got until about 4 o'clock in the afternoon.
I finally went in, and I popped it open, because I knew if it was really bad, I could go to a bar or something, you know, fix that problem for the night.
And I open it up, and he goes, dear David.
He goes, thank you so much.
Your products are absolutely amazing.
The best popcorn I've ever had.
And we made him some, we dried some sriracha sauce.
Oh, you actually made him some sriracha?
Yeah, I made him a popcorn, and I sent a picture of the bag.
He goes, we absolutely love it.
He goes, I want you to move forward.
He says, I have three requests.
One, I get to approve the packaging.
Two, you use my product in your popcorn.
